title: A beginner's guide to the Baroque
topic_path: A beginner's guide to Baroque art
A beginner's guide to the Baroque

assessment_items.question_content:
**The Counter-Reformation was a response to . . .**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The Counter-Reformation was the Catholic Church's effort to reaffirm its authority in light of the rise of Protestantism throughout Europe.

[Read this article to review](/humanities/monarchy-enlightenment/baroque-art1/beginners-guide-baroque1/a/baroque-art-in-europe-an-introduction)

choices.content: the growth of Protestantism.
the political instability in Italy.
the dominance of Catholic monarchs in France, Spain, and Portugal.
the development of a wealthy merchant class.

assessment_items.question_content:
**The rise of Protestantism led to all of the following EXCEPT . . .**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The printing press was invented before the Reformation. However, advancements in printing allowed for the spread of religious and scientific ideas throughout the 17th century. 

[Read this article to review](/humanities/monarchy-enlightenment/baroque-art1/beginners-guide-baroque1/a/baroque-art-in-europe-an-introduction)

choices.content: the development of the printing press.
the emergence of new subjects in visual art.
the rise of new patrons for the arts.
increased political turmoil.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Although Baroque art takes various forms, it is characterized by its attempt to . . . **

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Baroque artists experimented with ways to engage the viewer through increased realism and heightened illusionism.

[Read this article to review](/humanities/monarchy-enlightenment/baroque-art1/beginners-guide-baroque1/a/baroque-art-in-europe-an-introduction)

choices.content: involve the viewer
directly engage theological issues raised by the Protestant reformers.
provide critical commentary on society.
present images and ideas in a scientifically objective way.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was the main reason the *Royal Society for Improving Natural Knowledge* was established?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The Royal Society adhered to Bacon's principles of scientific investigation and shared its discoveries through a periodical publication.

[Read this article to review](/humanities/monarchy-enlightenment/baroque-art1/beginners-guide-baroque1/a/francis-bacon-and-the-scientific-revolution)

choices.content: to encourage and share scientific discoveries based on observation and experiment
to instruct artists and artisans in creating work inspired by nature
to promote religious doctrines as natural truths
to provide royal patronage for the arts and sciences

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which contributed the most to the spread of intellectual ideas in the 16th and 17th century?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Advancements in printing allowed for the spread of religious and scientific ideas throughout the 17th century.

[Read this article to review](/humanities/monarchy-enlightenment/baroque-art1/beginners-guide-baroque1/a/francis-bacon-and-the-scientific-revolution)

choices.content: advances in printing technology
the development of Bacon’s scientific method
the political stability across Europe
the development of a wealthy merchant class

assessment_items.question_content:
**The term Baroque derives from the Spanish and Portuguese word meaning . . .**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
18th century critics coined the term Baroque  from the Spanish and Portuguese word for an irregularly shaped pearl as a pejorative description of the period's art. 

[Read this article to review](/humanities/monarchy-enlightenment/baroque-art1/beginners-guide-baroque1/a/baroque-art-in-europe-an-introduction)

choices.content: an irregular pearl.
a smooth circular shell.
a theatrical prop.
a small concave mirror.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which idea was NOT promoted by Francis Bacon?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
As Bacon's philosophies on experimentation and observation came to be accepted, people began using them to harness nature for profit.

[Read this article for review](/humanities/monarchy-enlightenment/baroque-art1/beginners-guide-baroque1/a/francis-bacon-and-the-scientific-revolution)

choices.content: scientific discovery could further economic production
science should be based on empirical observation
experimentation was essential to the discovery of knowledge
scientific findings must be repeatable

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which was an outcome of the Scientific Revolution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Ideas developed through the Scientific Revolution of the 17th century gave way to the cultural and economic changes of the Industrial Revolution.  

[Read this article for review](/humanities/monarchy-enlightenment/baroque-art1/beginners-guide-baroque1/a/francis-bacon-and-the-scientific-revolution)

choices.content: economic growth due to industrialization
emphasis on textual sources as the foundations of human knowledge
the spread of religious ideas associated with Protestantism
increased artistic patronage among the middle classes

assessment_items.question_content:
**In the Dutch Republic in the 17th century . . . **

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Protestants in the Dutch republic in the 17th century rejected the large scale religious art made for churches more common in Catholic areas of Europe and the popularity of landscape paintings, portraits and genre scenes grew (genre paintings show scenes from every day life). These popular paintings were purchased to decorate homes and civic spaces (not churches). 

[Read this article to review](/humanities/monarchy-enlightenment/baroque-art1/beginners-guide-baroque1/a/baroque-art-in-europe-an-introduction)
The one answer that is incorrect is "the popularity of large-scale, Protestant-themed paintings for churches grew."

choices.content: the production of small scale paintings increased to decorate private homes.
the popularity of genre scenes grew.
the popularity of landscape painting grew.
the popularity of large-scale, Protestant-themed paintings for churches grew.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which is NOT a characteristic of Baroque art in the Dutch Republic?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Protestants in Northern Europe rejected the large-scale religious art common in Catholic churches.

[Read this article to review](/humanities/monarchy-enlightenment/baroque-art1/beginners-guide-baroque1/a/baroque-art-in-europe-an-introduction)

choices.content: the increase in production of small scale paintings to decorate private homes
the growing popularity of genre scenes
the growing popularity of landscapes
the growing popularity of large-scale, Protestant-themed paintings for churches

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did the Catholic Church differ in its ideas about religious art from the Protestants? **

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Protestants rejected the cult of images in the Catholic Church, concerned that images were being worshiped in themselves. Catholics believed that the visual arts could be used to guide, inspire, and communicate religious ideals to the faithful.

[Read this article to review](/humanities/monarchy-enlightenment/baroque-art1/beginners-guide-baroque1/a/baroque-art-in-europe-an-introduction)

choices.content: Protestants criticized what they saw as the Catholic cult of images.
Protestants believed art should be allowed only in churches.
Protestants believed images of saints should be venerated.
Protestants believed that images of Christ should be venerated, but not images of saints.
